And you want to clearly communicate what you expect from your contractors and when, to find what is and isn't okay when it comes to timeliness so that everyone is on the same page and then get them to tell you if it looks like a delay is likely to happen. And communication really is key and this is where most contractor arrangements break down and make sure that you set a clear communication channel for communications, whether that's email, Slack, Skype and stick to one channel so that it's clear. I'll give you an example. In my company, we all use Slack. No one sends anything via email, no one calls anyone, no one uses Skype, Facebook Messenger, text or any of that. It's just Slack. So all we know to check is Slack and we know if anyone wants something from us, they're going to use Slack. That way, by having a clear channel that we've set, we know that we only need to check one thing to know that we're good. So figure out what that is and stick to it. Now here's two pro tips. Pro tip number one is to get your contractors to write one sentence, one to two sentence daily updates at the end of each day stating what they worked on and whether they have any issues or questions. And this keeps the pulse and rhythm going. You want to get daily updates from your people every day and you just want to skim through them to make sure that they're all good and they've been doing work. All issues arise from day to day stuff. If an issue comes up, it would have shown signs day to day before it happened. And the awesome thing about daily updates is that you get to spot an issue before it even really becomes an issue because as soon as a daily update gets weird or goes missing, you know something's brewing there. And then tip number two is the way to gain control is to let go of it and observe.

## [12:13 - 15:14]

And you don't want to fall victim to micromanagement or try to run your team like an army commander. Give your team tons of freedom and a handful of rules. And their price won't really change if it's a five grand website or a 10 grand website. They should still be able to do the work from between $300 and $500. That's the price you should be willing to pay. And when it comes to a Facebook person or a Google AdWords person, really they're going to charge around $1,000 a month or in between $500 and $1,000 a month. So if you're charging two grand for Facebook and you just have to pay your contractor $1,000 and then you just keep $1,000, that's awesome because you keep $1,000 and you don't have to do any of the work.
